coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor detection of iodide ions in milk powder
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bingyan
He, Yanhua
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have successfully designed an “off-on” fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) system to detect iodide ions in milk powder. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was significantly quenched by Ag+ through forming N-GQDs/Ag+ complexes due to the binding between Ag+ and functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. However, with the addition of I− to the N-GQDs/Ag+ system, the fluorescence intensity was significantly restored because of the formation of AgI complexes by "hard and soft acids and bases principle"(HSAB) between Ag+ and I−. Under the optimal conditions, Ag+ had a good linear relationship in the range of 0.01–0.6 μM. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/ Ag+ linearly increased with the increasing of I− concentrations in the range of 0.0125–4.0 μM, and the detection limit (LOD) was 5.6 nM. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of milk powder, which is a detection system with good development prospects owing to convenience, rapidity and low toxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Fluorescent sensor
Silver ions
Iodide ions

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence intensity quenching of N-GQDs by Ag+ ions
Fluorescence intensity recovery of the N-GQDs/Ag+ system
Selectivity and interference of I− ions
The samples of I− ions be measured and processed
Determination of I− ions in milk powder

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) the N-GQDs and (b) the graphene quantum dots (GQDs); (B) (a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs (c) fluorescence excitation spectra of N-GQDs.
Fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths.
Effect of fluorescence intensity turn on of N-GQDs with different pH values.

The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs under different conditions (a) N-GQDs (60 μL, 0.10 g/L)+Ag+ (0.6 μM) ; (b) N-GQDs (60 μL, 0.10 g/L)+Ag+(0.6 μM)+I− (0.6 μM); (c) N-GQDs (60 μL, 0.10 g/L); (d) N-GQDs (60 μL, 0.10 g/L)+I− (0.6 μM).
(A) Fluorescence “turn off” of the N-GQDs with the addition of Ag+ ions; (B) Linear relative of fluorescence intensity in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ ions.
(A) Fluorescence “turn off” of the N-GQDs with the addition of I− ions; (B) Linear relative of fluorescence intensity in the presence of different concentrations of I− ions.
Selectivity and interference of N-GQDs/Ag+ system towards I− ions over other substances.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of iodide ion.
Detection of I− in milk powder (infant formula) samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor detection of iodide ions in milk powder

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation for N-GQDs and “off-on” fluorescence system of N-GQDs/Ag+- I−.

In this work, we have successfully designed an “off-on” fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) system to detect iodide ions in milk powder. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was significantly quenched by Ag+ through forming N-GQDs/Ag+ complexes due to the binding between Ag+ and functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. However, with the addition of I− to the N-GQDs/Ag+ system, the fluorescence intensity was significantly restored because of the formation of AgI complexes by "hard and soft acids and bases principle"(HSAB) between Ag+ and I−. Under the optimal conditions, Ag+ had a good linear relationship in the range of 0.01–0.6 μM. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/ Ag+ linearly increased with the increasing of I− concentrations in the range of 0.0125–4.0 μM, and the detection limit (LOD) was 5.6 nM. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of milk powder, which is a detection system with good development prospects owing to convenience, rapidity and low toxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic anion detection is a widely studied field of research, which plays an important role in environmental processes, industrial and biological processes [1–5]. Among various anionic ions, iodide ions have extraordinary significance which is a key factor in the control of metabolism in bone and brain development and also essential for the synthesis of thyroid hormones [6,7]. When the intake of iodide is insufficient, pregnant women will suffer spontaneous abortions, infant mortality and mental retardation. However, excessive dietary iodine can cause thyroid dysfunction [8,9]. As one of the necessary micronutrients for human growth, the development of analytical method for iodide ion has important research significance. To date, there is a wide range of determination of iodide ions have been reported. Anion exchange chromatography [10], microfluidic flow injection [11], colorimetry [12], the majority of these methods are sensitivity and have already developed for analyzing iodide ions with very good performance. Some methods for analyzing iodide ions by electrochemical have also been reported, such as capillary electrophoresis [13], potentiometric titration [14], electrochemical methods [15,16], however, electrochemical methods also has some disadvantages. For example, the coated of the electrodes membrane surface required cleaning or even replacement frequently, when dealing with complex real samples [17,18]. Extinction spectra [19] and fluorescence methods [20,21]. Among these, fluorescence methods not only have high sensitivity, but also simple sample preparation and rapid response. Based on these characteristics, this method has attracted people's extensive attention [22,23]. In recent years, public health has been devoted to reduce the intake of salt and lead to a reduction in the intake of iodine in iodized salt. In industrialized countries, milk and dairy products are important sources of dietary iodine [24]. However, there are different levels of iodine in milk and dairy products, making iodine intake uncontrollable, especially in infants who use milk powder as their main foods. Therefore, it is particularly important to detect the content of iodide ions in milk products such as milk powder.

As a late-model of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, graphitic quantum dots (GQDs) are widely concerned by scientists because of their good electronic and optical properties, as well as non-toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [25–27]. Due to its attractive properties, including photocatalysis [28], bioimaging [29] and medical diagnosis [30], GQDs had a wide range of applications. Although above, the non-specificity and low quantum yields (QYs) of GQDs have an adverse effect on that the selectivity and selectivity in the application of fluorescent probes [22,31,32]. In order to improve the performance of graphene quantum dots, we can be modified by doping other hetero-atoms, which increased the optical properties and quantum yield of graphene quantum dots [33]. In general, doped with hetero-atoms such as nitrogen [34,35], boron [36], sulfur [22], and chlorine [37] to effectively modify the properties of GQDs. Modified the surface of GQDs by doping N atoms to the material can improve its intrinsic properties and extend its application [38].

Herein, we synthesized N-GQDs by hydrothermal method using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source, and employed N-GQDs to achieve a fast, inexpensive, accurate way to detection iodide ions in infant milk powder, respectively. Ag+ ions exhibit affinity for oxygen groups and amino groups on the surface of N-GQDs to form the N-GQDs/Ag+ complex leading the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was obviously quenched caused by dissociated Ag+ ions, as termed “turn-off” effect. The fluorescence of the N-GQDs/ Ag+ system was restored by addition of I− which was attributed to the strong interaction between Ag+ ions and I− ions to form AgI complex by "hard and soft acids and bases principle"(HSAB), as named “turn-on” effect. Therefore the N-GQDs was developed as an "turn-on" fluorescent sensor for quantitative determination of I− ions.

Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (H2NCONH2) and other nitrate reagents were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Dihydrate Sodium phosphate monobasic dehydrate(NaH2PO4·2H2O) and Sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O were purchased from ShenTai Huagong Reagents Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China) All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ obtained from the United States Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was acquired on a LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 300 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 380 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron Corp., USA). Raman spectra were obtained on a Renishaw (UK). TEM images were acquired on a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The crystal structure of QDs was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XPS) on an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) was performed on an Ultima IV (Rigaku, Japan) spectrometer equipped with monochromatized Al Kα excitation.
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were prepared from the previous work [39]. Details of this method can be found in Supplementary Data.

The concentration of N-GQDs was diluted to 0.10 g/L with purified water. In order to study the quenching effect of Ag+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs, 60 μL N-GQDs solution and 250 μL PBS buffer solution(0.2 M, pH = 7.0) was added into a series of AgNO3 solution with different concentrations at room temperature. After reaction for 10 min and diluted to 5 mL with purified water, we collected the fluorescence spectra from 380 nm to 630 nm, and where the fluorescence was adjusted to an excitation wavelength was adjusted to of 365 nm. At that time, slit widths of excitation were 5 nm and emission slit widths were 10 nm.

For the assay of I− ions, 60 μL N-GQDs solution was added into a 10 mL calibrated test tube solution. Subsequently, 60 μL, 0.05 mM Ag+ ions and 250 μL PBS buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 7.0) were mixed for 10 min. Then, a different concentration of I− ions were added into the above solution and reaction for 10 min. The volume of mixture was adjusted to 5 mL with purified water. The fluorescence emission spectra were measure and recorded (Scheme 1 ).

Fig. 1 (A) illustrated UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs and GQDs, respectively. The GQDs exhibit a typical absorption at 360 nm. Nevertheless, a very well-defined absorption peak of N-GQDs could be observed at 330 nm and displayed blue shift of 30 nm, which compared with the graphene quantum dots. The phenomena indicated that the incorporation of nitrogen atoms could be successfully into the GQDs by the present synthetic process. Fig. 1 (B) shows that the excitation wavelength of N-GQDs is 365 nm and the emission wavelength is 445 nm. As shown in Fig.2 the fluorescence emission wavelength of N-GQDs displayed nearly no shift when the excitation wavelength changed in the range of 300–390 nm and the intensity reached the maximum at 445 nm upon excitation at 390 nm. Above all, we can conclude that the N-GQDs were excitation-independent.

As shown in Fig. S1 (A) N-GQDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM), which showed that the fairly uniform and well distributed of N-GQDs were intuitively displayed, and Fig. S1 (B) shown that N-GQDs exhibited an average diameter of 4.6 ± 0.6 nm. In the XRD pattern (Fig. S1 (C)), the XRD peaks of N-GQDs shifted to higher positions (2Θ = 27.5°) as compared to the graphite structure (2Θ = 23.5°), indicating that the oxygen and nitrogen atoms were successfully doped in N-GQDs [40]. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.92. As we all know, the relative intensity of the D band is a reflection of the degree of disorder of the crystal structure, and the G band represents the first order scattering E2g vibration mode, which is used to characterize the sp2 bond structure of carbon. The intensity ratio of ID/IG is used to measure the disorder in graphene materials. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.92, which means that the introduction of N atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone induced numerous GQDs defects. In order to further explore the structure of N-GQDs, FTIR spectrum characterization was performed (Fig. S1 (D) (curves b)). The peaks at 3438 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of OH / NH and C–O, respectively. The 2926 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C–H, and there is a stretching vibration peak of C–N at 1402 cm−1, a stretching vibration peak of C–H at 1047 cm−1. These results indicate the successful synthesis of N-GQDs.

In order to explore the surface functional groups of N-GQDs, XPS characterization was also utilized. The existence of carbon (C 1 s, 285.1 eV), nitrogen (N 1 s, 400.8 eV) and oxygen (O 1 s, 531.7 eV) of N-GQDs was showed in Fig. S2 (A). As shown in Fig. S2 (B), the high resolution C 1 s revealed two peaks at 284.8 and 288.8 eV which are attributed to the binding energy of carbon in CC, C–N/C–O, and C＝O, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra showed that there were two relative nitrogen species of pyrrolic N (C–NC) (399.6 eV) and graphitic N or NH bands (400.3 eV) in Fig.S2 (B). The O 1s peak could be fitted peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.5 eV into C＝O and COH/COC groups(Fig. S3D). The above mentioned were consistent with FRIT characterization, we can concluded that N-GQDs have been successfully synthesized. The typical absorption peaks at around 330 nm, 360 nm and 400 nm could be assigned to the electron transitions from π (or n) to π* of C＝C, C＝N and C＝O, respectively Fig.1. (B). Taking quinine sulfate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%) as the calculated reference standard, we obtain that the quantum yield of N-GQDs was about 15.3%, which was consistent with our previous report [41].

As shown in Fig.3 , the pH(from pH 5.0–9.0, PBS buffer)of the solution has an impacted on the fluorescence quenching efficiency by Ag+ ions (Effq) and the fluorescence recovery efficiency by I− ions (Effr). The efficiencies were computed by the following equations, respectively:
(1) Effq(%) = (F0 − F)/F0
(2) Effr(%) = (Fr − F)/(F0 − F)
Where F0 and F indicate fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs at 445 nm in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions, respectively; Fr represent recovered fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs at 445 nm in the presence of I− ions at the same pH. The Effq gradually increased as pH value from 5.0 to 7.0, however, when the pH value was greater than 7.0, the Effq has a slight degradation. Since the amino groups on the surface of N-GQDs were protonated at a pH in the range of 5.0 to 7.0, the coordination of Ag+ ions with amino groups was adversely affected. However, when pH was higher than 7.0, the deprotonation of the amino group makes the coordination between Ag+ ions and N-GQDs stronger and thus the Effq was increased. As pH value (range of pH 5.0 to 9.0) increased, Effr was gradually decreased. Because of Ag+ ions’ belonging to soft acids, I− ions as a soft base would be more easily combined with Ag+ ions under acidic conditions. According to the above results, when pH = 7.0 Effq and Effr were close and to an optimal pH for experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence intensity quenching of N-GQDs by Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 . (comparison between curves a and c), after the addition of Ag+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs can be observed to dramatically decreased to 69.3% with the concentration of Ag+ ions was 0.6 μM. We collected the fluorescence spectra from 380 nm to 630 nm, and where the fluorescence was adjusted to an excitation wavelength was adjusted to of 365 nm. Under the same conditions, after added Ag+ ions to GQDs, there is no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs (Fig.S3). In accordance with Fig. S1 (d)(curves b), compared to the FTIR of N-GQDs, the absorption band at 1154 cm−1,1079 cm−1,666 cm−1 and 530 cm−1 of N-GQDs/Ag+ may indicate the formation of Ag-O bonds and Ag-N bonds. So, we speculated the reason for the fluorescence quenching is that Ag+ ions have the affinity for O and N which can be to form a complex with the oxygen groups and amino groups on the surface of N-GQDs.

Under optimum conditions, we recorded that the fluorescence intensity of the different concentrations of Ag+ ions which after added addition to N-GQDs (60 μL, 0.10 g/L) and fluorescence was collected at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As illustrated in Fig.5 A, since the decreasing fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs system cause by the addition of Ag+ ions was proportional to the Ag+ ions concentration. The concentration of Ag+ ions in the range of 0.01–0.08 μM, the linear regression equation was (F0-F)/F0 = 1.8486[Ag+]+0.08252 (R2 = 0.99742). When the concentration of Ag+ ions in the range of 0.08–0.6 μM, the linear regression equation was (F0-F)/F0 = 0.89608[Ag+]+0.1697 (R2 = 0.99438). F0 and F indicate fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ ions, respectively. The fluorescence intensity quenching of N-GQDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Ag+ ions, and the concentration range of Ag+ ions was 0.01 μM-0.6 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence intensity recovery of the N-GQDs/Ag+ system
ce:section.ce:para: When we added I− ions to the system, the fluorescence intensity quenched by Ag+ ions were effectively recovered (Fig.4. curves b), which means the binding preference between Ag+ ions and I− ions were strong. In order to confirm that I− ions was added to the N-GQDs solution alone, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs was rarely slightly changed (Fig.4. comparison between curves c and d). So, the phenomenon of fluorescence intensity recovered was explained by "hard and soft acids and bases principle"(HSAB) [42]. In HSAB, Ag+ ions belong to soft acids and I− ions belong to soft bases. According to HSAB, soft acids can form to strong interaction with soft bases, which leads to Ag+ ions showing a strong binding force to I− ions and the AgI complex was formed.

To ensure that the development system was able to determine the sensitivity of I− ions, recorded the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/Ag+ system in the presence of I− ions. As appeared in Fig.4A, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/Ag+ system gradually increased as the content of I− ions grew. So, we founded a linear relationship between I− ions concentration and fluorescence intensity (Fig.6 B). It was clear that I− ions in the concentrations ranged from 0.0125 to 0.35 μM showed a good linear correlation, the linear regression equation was (FR-FR0)/FR0 = 3.0024[I−]+0.1569, (R2 = 0.9981).The I− ions detection ranges of 0.35–4.0 μM and the linear equation was (FR-FR0)/FR0 = 0.1969[I−]+1.1186, (R2 = 0.9981). FR0 and FR indicate fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs/Ag+ system in the absence and presence of I− ions, respectively. The detection limit of this method is 5.6 nM. Compared with the different methods of detecting iodide ions, as listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the N-GQDs/Ag+ detection system we developed is simpler and less time consuming. In contrast to the fluorescence methods [20], the method we have established has a lower detection limit and wider detection ranges. By contrast, although the linear range of method [21] is slightly wider, we developed system shows lower detection limit. In this work, we use N-GQDs, not only the synthesis method of that simpler, but the quantum yield has been greatly improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of I− ions
ce:section.ce:para: For the real samples, a variety of substances will cause potential risks for the experimental system. Specificity is one of the important indicators of the experiment which measures the interference and selectivity of the experimental system. The selectivity of the N-GQDs/Ag+ system for the detection of I− ions was further evaluated and other interfering substances were added. First, 0.225 μM of I− ions and other substances (including 22.5 μM of NO3 −, CIO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2-, C2O4 2-, AA, glucose, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+; 11.25 μM of SO4 2-, CO3 2-, HCO3 −, Ni2+, K+,Mg2+; 2.25 μM of F−, Br−, CI−; 0.225 μM SCN−) were added into N-GQDs solution respectively (fluorescence spectra with other interference ions as shown in Fig. S4). The black bars in the Fig.7 show the efficiency (Fr-F)/(F0-F) of adding different ions for fluorescence recovery of the N-GQDs/Ag+ quenching system, fluorescence recovery efficiency value of other substances were not obvious compared with I- ions. The binding ability of other substances with Ag+ ions is weaker than the binding force of Ag+ ions and I− ions, and the fluorescence can not be recovered obviously after addition. Second, explore interfering experiments, 0.225 μM of I− ions was mixed with the above-mentioned various concentrations of substances and added to N-GQDs/Ag+ quenching system. Fluorescence recovery efficiency values in the presence of interference were represented by red bars. N-GQDs/Ag+ system fluorescence did not significantly affect. This method of detecting I− ions has excellent selectivity and specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The samples of I− ions be measured and processed
ce:section.ce:para: All of milk powder (infant formula) as actual samples were purchased from local supermarkets. According to the instructions, the 15 g of milk powder with purified water after stirred and dissolved into a volume of 100 mL. Acetic acid solution was added to the milk powder to adjust the pH to 4.6, and then centrifuged at 8000 r for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μM filtered membrane, after suitable dilution (1:10, v/v), the real samples were introduced to the fluorescent system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of I− ions in milk powder
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy and reliability of the detection method can be evaluated by the recovery study, which is implemented on samples spiked with I− ions solutions and milk powder has been processed was measured by fluorescence sensor. The I− ions content in milk powder for infant formula was calculated, as can be seen in Table 2 , the recoveries varied were range from 92.09% to 103.49% with the reproducibility(RSD%, n = 3)was below 1.95%. These results demonstrating that the fluorescence sensor we developed was high accuracy, reproducible and suitable for determination of I− ions in milk powder.

In summary, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was significantly reduced when Ag+ ions were added. After addition of I− ions, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced due to HSAB. Based on these phenomena, we have established a fluorescence sensing system which detection I− ions. Under the optimized conditions, a low detection limit and a good linear range can be obtained. Moreover, the detection method developed in this work is simple, eco-friendly and does not involve expensive instruments and reagents. So, the method with prompt detection of silver ions in water and iodine ions in milk powder we development in this work will be promising candidates for on-site analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.09.014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
